365	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
366	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
367	on their proof sketches.
368	
369	\begin{lemma}
370	
371	\leanok
372	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
373	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
374	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
375	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
376	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
377	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
378	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
379	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
380	  itself.
381	\end{lemma}
382	
383	\begin{proof}
384	
385	
386	
387	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
388	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
389	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
390	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
391	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
392	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
393	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
394	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
395	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
396	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
398	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
399	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
400	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
401	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
402	  basic-open computation below.
403	\end{proof}
404	
405	\begin{lemma}
406	
407	\leanok
408	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
409	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
411	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
412	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
413	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
414	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
415	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
416	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
417	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
418	\end{lemma}
419	
420	\begin{proof}
421	
422	
423	
424	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
425	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
426	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
427	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
428	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
429	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
430	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
431	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
432	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
433	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
434	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
435	\end{proof}
436	
437	\begin{lemma}
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
441	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
443	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
444	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
445	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
446	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
447	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
448	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
449	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
450	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
451	  \[
452	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;

454	  \]
455	\end{lemma}
456	
457	\begin{proof}
458	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
459	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
460	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),

470	
471	\begin{lemma}\leanok
472	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
473	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
474	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
475	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
476	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
477	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
478	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
479	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
480	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
481	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
482	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
483	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
484	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
485	% is hand-proved per a.
486	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
487	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
488	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
489	  \[
490	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
491	    \;\cong\;
492	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
493	  \]
494	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
495	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
496	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
497	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
498	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
499	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
500	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
501	
502	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
503	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
504	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
505	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
506	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
507	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
508	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
509	\end{lemma}
510	
511	\begin{proof}
512	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
513	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
514	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
515	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
516	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
517	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
518	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
519	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
520	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
521	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
522	
523	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
524	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
525	  \[
526	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
527	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
528	  \]
529	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
530	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
531	
532	  \medskip
533	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
534	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
535	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
536	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
537	
538	  \medskip
539	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
540	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
541	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
542	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
543	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
544	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
545	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
546	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
547	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
548	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
549	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
550	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
551	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
552	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
553	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
554	  an isomorphism.
555	
556	  \medskip
557	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
558	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
559	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
560	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
561	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
562	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
563	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
564	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
565	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
566	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
567	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
568	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
569	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
570	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
571	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
572	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
573	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
574	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
575	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
576	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
577	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
578	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
579	  movements.
580	
581	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
582	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
584	  \[
585	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
586	      \;\cong\;
587	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
588	  \]
589	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
590	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
591	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
592	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
593	
594	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
595	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
596	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
597	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
598	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
599	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
600	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
601	  \[
602	    \begin{array}{ccc}
603	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
604	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
605	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
606	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
607	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
608	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
609	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
610	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
611	    \end{array}
612	  \]
613	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
614	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
615	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
616	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
617	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
618	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
619	  \(\widetilde M\).
620	
621	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
622	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
623	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
624	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
625	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
626	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
627	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
628	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
629	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
630	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
631	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
632	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
633	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
634	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
635	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
636	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
637	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
638	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
639	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
640	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
641	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
642	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
643	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
644	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
645	
646	  \medskip
647	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
648	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
649	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
650	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
651	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
652	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
653	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
654	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
655	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
656	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
657	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
658	  naturality square for the inclusion
659	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
660	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
661	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
662	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
663	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
664	
665	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
666	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
667	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
668	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
669	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
670	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
671	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
672	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
673	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
674	
675	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
676	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
677	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
678	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
679	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
680	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
681	  % bump.
682	  \medskip
683	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
684	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
685	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
686	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
687	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
688	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
689	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
690	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
691	  needed for the affine lane.
692	\end{proof}
693	
694	\begin{remark}
695	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
696	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
697	  \begin{enumerate}
698	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
699	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
700	      \(R\)-module of sections of
701	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
702	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
703	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
704	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
705	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
706	      closed under isomorphism; hence
707	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
708	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
709	      needed for the affine lane.
710	  \end{enumerate}
711	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
712	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
713	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
714	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
715	\end{remark}
716	
717	\subsection{The pullback companion}
718	
719	The affine reduction below requires, alongside the pushforward dictionary, the
720	\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
721	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
722	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.
723	
724	The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints from the
725	per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison of
726	Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, packaged as a natural isomorphism of
727	right-adjoint functors (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}).
728	
729	\begin{lemma}
730	
731	\leanok
732	[Naturality packaging of the pushforward \(\Gamma\)-comparison]
733	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardNatIso}
735	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
736	  The per-object comparison of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} assembles into a
737	  natural isomorphism of the two right-adjoint functors
738	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)}\) and
739	  \(\widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) on modules, exhibiting the
740	  pushforward dictionary as a natural transformation rather than merely a family of
741	  object isomorphisms. This is the input to the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints argument
742	  that produces the pullback companion Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}.
743	\end{lemma}
744	\begin{proof}
745	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
746	  The components are the object isomorphisms of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso};
747	  naturality is the corresponding naturality square, which holds because every
748	  constituent of the comparison is identity-on-elements.
749	\end{proof}
750	
751	\begin{lemma}
752	
753	\leanok
754	[Affine pullback of a tilde-module]
755	  \label{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
756	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
757	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
758	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma `lemma-widetilde-pullback', Tag 01I9
759	  % (\S\,Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
760	  % L1241--1256).
761	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let
762	  % $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
763	  % $(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
764	  % be affine schemes.
765	  % Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
766	  % morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
767	  % $\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
768	  % \begin{enumerate}
769	  % \item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
770	  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
771	  % \item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
772	  % in the $S$-module $N$.
773	  % \end{enumerate}"
774	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Lemma ``\(\widetilde M\) and pullback''
775	  (Tag 01I9), part (1).}
776	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
777	  be an \(R\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
778	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-modules
779	  \[
780	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\,\widetilde{M}
781	    \;\cong\;
782	    \widetilde{\bigl(R' \otimes_R M\bigr)},
783	  \]
784	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
785	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
786	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
787	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
788	  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
789	  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
790	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
791	  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine
792	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, and is part~(1)
793	  of the same Stacks lemma of which the pushforward formula is part~(2).
794	\end{lemma}
795	
796	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The first assertion follows from the identification in
797	% Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
798	% and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}."
799	\begin{proof}
800	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
801	  Pullback along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, on quasi-coherent sheaves, the
802	  extension-of-scalars operation \(- \otimes_R R'\) on modules transported through
803	  the \(\widetilde{(-)}\) / \(\operatorname{moduleSpec}\Gamma\) equivalence between
804	  modules and quasi-coherent sheaves. Concretely, the global sections of
805	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M}\) are the base change
806	  \(R' \otimes_R M\): the pullback functor \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{*}\) is
807	  left adjoint to the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\), and under
808	  the global-sections identification the latter is restriction of scalars along
809	  \(\varphi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}); the left adjoint of
810	  restriction of scalars is extension of scalars \(M \mapsto R' \otimes_R M\). The
811	  comparison map is the unit of the base-change adjunction, and the universal
812	  property of base change identifies it with the canonical isomorphism
813	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M} \cong
814	  \widetilde{(R' \otimes_R M)}\). Naturality in \(M\) is the naturality of that
815	  unit. (One may equally read this off the standard-open description: on
816	  \(D(\varphi a)\) the localization \((R' \otimes_R M)[1/a]\) is
817	  \(R'[1/\varphi a] \otimes_{R[1/a]} M[1/a]\), which is the base change of the
818	  sections of \(\widetilde M\) over \(D(a)\).)
819	\end{proof}

821	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
822	
823	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
824	reduces, via the locality criterion
825	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
826	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
827	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
828	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
829	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
830	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
831	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
832	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
833	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
834	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
835	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
836	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
837	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
838	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
839	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
840	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
841	
842	\begin{lemma}
843	
844	\leanok
845	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
846	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
847	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
848	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
849	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
850	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
851	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
852	  % L920--926).
853	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
854	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
855	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
856	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
857	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
858	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
859	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
860	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of the
861	  base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible
862	  with restriction to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
863	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
864	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
865	  by the affine-open locality criterion
866	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
867	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
868	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
869	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
870	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
871	
872	\end{lemma}
873	
874	\begin{proof}
875	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
876	  By the affine-open locality criterion
877	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the base-change map is an isomorphism
878	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules as soon as its restriction to the sections over each
879	  affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
880	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
881	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
882	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
883	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
884	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
885	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
886	
887	  \medskip
888	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
889	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, the base-change map is the image,
890	  under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
891	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
892	  \[
893	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
894	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
895	  \]
896	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
897	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
898	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
899	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
900	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
901	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
902	
903	  \medskip
904	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
905	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
906	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
907	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
908	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
909	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
910	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
911	  \begin{itemize}
912	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
913	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
914	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
915	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
916	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
917	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
918	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
919	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
920	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
921	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
922	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
923	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
924	  \end{itemize}
925	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
926	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
927	  restricted counterpart — shows that the restriction of the base-change map to
928	  sections over \(U\) is itself the base-change map formed from the cartesian square
929	  restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
930	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
931	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
932	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
933	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
934	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
935	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
936	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
937	  obligation beyond them.
938	
939	  \medskip
940	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
941	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
942	  that the base-change map restricts to an isomorphism on sections over each affine
943	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2, precisely the
944	  affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with \emph{no} flatness
945	  hypothesis, by the section-level computation
946	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
947	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
948	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion
949	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} to this family of per-open
950	  isomorphisms shows that the base-change map is an isomorphism, as asserted.
951	\end{proof}
952	
953	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
954	
955	The section-level identification
956	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
957	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close, and we prove it \emph{directly on
958	global sections}, with no abstract adjoint-mate tower. In the affine--affine model the
959	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
960	proved tilde dictionaries, exactly the \(R'\)-base change of the algebraic unit of
961	extension of scalars; post-composing with the canonical regrouping isomorphism
962	\((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) identifies it with the canonical
963	base-change comparison, an isomorphism carrying no flatness hypothesis. The computation
964	factors through three named reads — the domain read, the codomain read, and the single
965	\emph{section-level identity} that the conjugated map is the base change of the unit —
966	together with the one Mathlib-absent crux identifying the pullback cone legs with the
967	\(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps of the tensor inclusions
968	(Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}). Two Mathlib results are recorded
969	first as dependency anchors.
970

972	  \label{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	\end{theorem}
65	
66	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
67	% exactly this signature):
68	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
69	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
70	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
71	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
72	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
73	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
76	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
77	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
78	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
79	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
80	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
81	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
82	% there exists a finite filtration
83	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
84	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
85	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
86	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
87	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
88	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
89	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
90	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
91	\begin{proof}
92	
93	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
94	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
95	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
96	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
97	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
98	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
99	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
100	
101	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
102	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
103	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
104	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
105	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
106	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
107	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
108	  for exactly this residue.
109	
110	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
111	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
112	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
113	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
114	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
115	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
116	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
117	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
118	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
119	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
120	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
121	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
122	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
123	\end{proof}
124	
125	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
126	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
127	
128	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
129	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
130	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
131	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
132	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
133	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
134	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
135	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
136	
137	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
138	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
140	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
141	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
142	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	
147	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
148	  finitely presented, so
149	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
150	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
151	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
152	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
153	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
154	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
155	  \(f := r\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
159	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
160	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
161	  \mathlibok
162	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
163	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
164	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
165	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
166	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
167	\end{lemma}
168	
169	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
170	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
172	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
173	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
174	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
175	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
176	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
177	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
178	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
179	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
180	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
181	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
182	  induction).}
183	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
184	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
185	  \(A_f\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
191	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
192	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
193	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
194	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
195	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
196	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
197	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
198	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
202	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
203	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
204	  \mathlibok
205	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
206	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
207	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
208	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
209	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
210	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
211	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
212	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
213	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
214	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
215	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
216	\end{lemma}
217	
218	\begin{lemma}[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
219	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
221	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
222	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
223	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
224	  exact sequence
225	  \[
226	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
227	  \]
228	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
229	\end{lemma}
230	
231	\begin{proof}
232	
233	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
234	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
235	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence. Proved directly in Lean.
236	\end{proof}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
239	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
241	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
242	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
243	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
244	\end{lemma}
245	
246	\begin{proof}
247	
248	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
249	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
250	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
251	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
252	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
253	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
254	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
258	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
260	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
261	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
262	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	
267	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
268	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
269	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
273	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
275	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
276	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
278	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
279	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
280	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
281	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
282	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
283	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
284	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
285	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
286	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
288	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
289	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
290	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
291	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
296	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
298	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
299	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
300	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
301	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
302	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
303	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
304	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
305	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
306	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
307	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
308	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
309	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
310	  free over \(A_f\).
311	\end{proof}

313	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
314	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
315	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
316	  \mathlibok
317	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
318	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
319	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
320	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
321	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
322	\end{lemma}

324	\begin{lemma}
325	
326	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
327	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
329	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
330	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
331	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
332	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
333	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
334	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
336	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
338	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
339	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
340	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
341	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
342	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
343	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
344	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
345	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
346	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
347	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
348	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
349	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
350	  %         p ∈ Set.range
351	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
352	  % NOTE (iter-008 encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
353	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
354	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
355	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
356	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
357	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
358	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
359	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
360	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
361	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
362	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
363	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
364	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
365	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
371	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
372	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
373	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
374	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
375	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
376	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
377	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
378	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
379	\end{proof}
380	
381	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
382	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
383	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
384	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
385	  % NOTE (resolved iter-006): the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
386	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
387	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
388	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
389	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
390	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
391	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
392	  % The conclusion is:
393	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
394	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
395	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
396	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
397	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
398	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
399	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
400	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
401	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
402	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
403	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
404	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
405	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
406	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
407	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
408	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
409	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
410	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
411	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
412	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
413	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
414	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
415	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
416	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
418	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
419	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
420	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
421	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
422	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
423	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
424	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
425	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
426	  normalisation with denominators).}
427	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
428	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
429	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
430	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
431	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
432	  \[
433	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
434	  \]
435	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
436	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
437	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
438	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
439	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
444	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
445	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
446	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
447	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
448	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
449	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
450	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
451	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
452	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
453	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
454	
455	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
456	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
457	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
458	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
459	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
460	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
461	
462	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
463	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
464	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
465	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
466	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
467	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
468	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
469	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
470	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
471	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
472	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
473	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
474	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
475	
476	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
477	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
478	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
479	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
480	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
481	  image of \(\varphi\).
482	\end{proof}
483	
484	\begin{lemma}
485	
486	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
487	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
489	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
490	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
491	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
492	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
493	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
494	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
495	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
496	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
497	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
498	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
499	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
500	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
501	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
502	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
503	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
504	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
505	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
506	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
507	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
508	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
509	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
510	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
511	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
512	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
513	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
514	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
515	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
516	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
517	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
518	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
519	  \[
520	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
521	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
522	  \]
523	
524	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
525	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
526	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
527	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
528	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
529	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{proof}
533	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
534	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
535	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
536	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
537	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
538	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
539	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
540	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
541	
542	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
543	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
544	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
545	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
546	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
547	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
548	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
549	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
550	
551	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
552	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
553	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
554	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
555	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
556	\end{proof}

568	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
569	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
570	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
571	  \mathlibok
572	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
573	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
574	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
575	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
576	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
577	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
578	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
579	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
580	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
581	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
582	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
583	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
584	\end{lemma}
585	
586	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
587	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
588	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
589	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
590	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
591	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
592	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
593	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
594	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
595	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
596	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
597	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
598	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
599	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
600	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
601	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
602	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
603	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
604	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
605	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
606	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
607	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
608	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
609	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
610	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
611	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
612	\end{lemma}
613	
614	\begin{proof}
615	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
616	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
617	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
618	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
619	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
620	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
621	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
622	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
623	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
624	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
625	\end{proof}
