5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
347	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
349	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
350	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
351	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
352	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
353	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
354	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
355	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
357	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
358	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
359	  %  function
360	  %  $$
361	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
362	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
363	  %  $$
364	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
365	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
366	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
367	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
368	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
369	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
370	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
371	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
372	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
373	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
374	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
375	  such that for all \(n > N\)
376	  \[
377	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
378	  \]
379	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
380	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
381	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
382	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
383	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
384	
385	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
386	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
387	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
388	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
389	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
390	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
391	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
392	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
393	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
394	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
395	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
396	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
397	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
398	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
399	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
400	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
404	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
405	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality infrastructure (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is
406	  %   formalized and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration, but
407	  %   it is no longer blocked on a Mathlib gap: the ambient subquotient route
408	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps the missing graded-quotient /
409	  %   graded-kernel / regrading API by keeping every object an ambient family
410	  %   `fun n => Naux ⊓ ℳ n` of submodules of the fixed `M`. No `DirectSum.Decomposition`
411	  %   on a subtype `↥p` or quotient `M ⧸ p` carrier is ever constructed, so the
412	  %   `DirectSum.IsInternal` / `Submodule.map_iSup` `isDefEq`/`whnf` runaway cannot fire.
413	  %   The residual is the ambient bookkeeping build of the subquotient blocks below.
414	\end{lemma}
415	
416	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
417	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
418	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
419	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
420	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
421	%  following short exact sequence
422	%  $$
423	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
424	%  $$
425	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
426	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
427	\begin{proof}
428	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
429	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
430	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
431	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
432	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
433	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
434	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
435	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
436	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
437	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
438	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
439	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
440	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
441	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
442	
443	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
444	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
445	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
446	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
447	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
448	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
449	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
450	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
451	  is the number on which we induct.
452	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
453	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
454	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
455	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
456	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
457	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
458	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
459	
460	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
461	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
462	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
463	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
464	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
465	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
466	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
467	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
468	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
469	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
470	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
471	
472	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
473	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
474	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
475	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
476	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
477	  \[
478	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
479	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
480	  \]
481	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
482	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
483	  with
484	  \[
485	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
486	    \qquad (n > N),
487	  \]
488	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
489	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
490	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
491	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
492	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
493	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
494	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
495	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
496	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
497	\end{proof}
498	
499	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
500	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
558	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
559	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
560	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
561	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
562	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
563	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
564	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
565	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
566	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
567	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
568	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
569	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
570	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
571	\end{proof}
572	
573	\begin{definition}\leanok
574	[Rational Hilbert function]
575	  \label{def:ratHilb}
576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
577	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
578	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
579	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
580	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
581	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
582	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
583	  \[
584	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
585	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
586	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
587	  \]
588	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
589	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
590	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
591	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
592	\end{definition}
593	
594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
595	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
596	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
599	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
600	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
601	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
602	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
603	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
604	\end{lemma}

607	[Raising the pole order]
608	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}

612	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
613	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
614	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
615	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
616	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
617	\end{lemma}

643	\begin{lemma}\leanok
644	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
645	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
647	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
648	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
649	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
650	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
651	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
652	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
653	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
654	\end{lemma}

656	\begin{proof}
657	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
658	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
659	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
660	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
661	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
662	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
663	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
664	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
665	\end{proof}

667	\begin{lemma}\leanok
668	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
669	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
671	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
672	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
673	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
674	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
675	  \[
676	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
677	  \]
678	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
679	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
680	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
681	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
682	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
683	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
684	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
685	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
686	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
687	\end{lemma}
688	
689	\begin{proof}
690	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
691	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
692	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
693	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
694	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
695	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
696	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
697	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
698	\end{proof}

700	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
701	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
703	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
704	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
705	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
706	  % Polynomials" (read from
707	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
709	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
710	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
711	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
712	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
713	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
714	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
715	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
716	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
717	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
718	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
719	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
720	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
721	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
722	  \[
723	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
724	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
725	  \]
726	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
727	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
728	\end{theorem}
729	
730	\begin{proof}
731	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
732	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
733	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
734	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
735	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
736	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
737	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
738	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
739	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
740	  function
741	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
742	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
743	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
744	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
745	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
746	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
747	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
748	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
749	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
750	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
751	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
752	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
753	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
754	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
755	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
756	\end{proof}

758	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
759	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
760	
761	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
762	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
763	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
764	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
765	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
766	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
767	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
768	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
769	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
770	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
771	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
772	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
773	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
774	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
775	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
776	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
777	
778	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
779	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
780	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
781	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
782	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
783	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
784	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
785	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
786	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
787	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
788	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
789	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
790	
791	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
792	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
793	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
794	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
795	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
796	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
797	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
798	\(x \in R_1\).
799	
800	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
801	
802	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
803	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
804	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
805	  \mathlibok
806	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
807	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
808	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
809	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
810	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
811	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
812	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
813	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
814	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
815	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
816	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
817	\end{lemma}
818	
819	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
820	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
821	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
822	  \mathlibok
823	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
824	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
825	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
826	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
827	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
828	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
829	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
830	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
831	\end{lemma}

833	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
834	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
835	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
836	  \mathlibok
837	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
838	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
839	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
840	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
841	  \[
842	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
843	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
844	  \]
845	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
846	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
847	\end{lemma}

849	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
850	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
851	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
852	  \mathlibok
853	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
854	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
855	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
856	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
857	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,

863	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
864	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
865	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
866	  \mathlibok
867	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}

879	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}

890	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots,

896	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
897	
898	\begin{lemma}\leanok
899	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
900	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
901	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
902	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
903	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
904	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
905	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
906	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
907	  \(n\),
908	  \[
909	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
910	  \]
911	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
912	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
913	\end{lemma}
914	
915	\begin{proof}
916	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
917	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
918	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
919	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
920	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
921	\end{proof}
922	
923	\begin{lemma}\leanok
924	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
925	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
926	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
927	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
928	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
929	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
930	  \[
931	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
932	  \]
933	  Together with the independence of
934	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
935	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
936	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
937	\end{lemma}
938	
939	\begin{proof}
940	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
941	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
942	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
943	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
944	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
945	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
946	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
947	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
948	\end{proof}

1227	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1228	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1229	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1230	% used in the codomain-read computation.

1232	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1233	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1234	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1236	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1237	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1238	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1239	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1240	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1241	\end{lemma}
1242	\begin{proof}
1243	
1244	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1245	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1246	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1247	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1248	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1249	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1250	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1251	\end{proof}
1252	
1253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1254	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1255	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1257	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1258	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1259	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1260	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1261	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1262	\end{lemma}
1263	\begin{proof}
1264	
1265	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1266	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1267	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1268	\end{proof}

1274	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1275	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1276	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1278	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1279	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1280	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1281	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1282	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1283	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1284	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1285	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1286	  % L933--938).
1287	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1288	  % equality
1289	  % $$
1290	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1291	  % $$
1292	  % as $R'$-modules."
1293	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1294	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1295	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1296	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1297	  \[
1298	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1299	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1300	  \]
1301	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1302	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1303	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1304	  hypothesis.
1305	\end{lemma}
1306	
1307	\begin{proof}
1308	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1309	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1310	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1311	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1312	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1313	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1314	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1315	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1316	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1317	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1318	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1319	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1320	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1321	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1322	
1323	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1324	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1325	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1326	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1327	  dictionaries,
1328	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1329	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1330	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1331	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1332	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1333	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1334	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1335	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1336	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1337	  \[
1338	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1339	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1340	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1341	  \]
1342	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1343	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1344	
1345	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1346	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1347	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1348	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1349	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1350	\end{proof}

1352	% --- Section-level identity, decomposed into three linked steps ---

1389	  Let \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) be the canonical inclusion
1390	  (\(a \mapsto a \otimes 1\)). The unit of the
1391	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\)-adjunction
1392	  evaluated at the affine sheaf \(\widetilde M\), read on the global sections over
1393	  \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) through the tilde dictionaries
1394	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) and
1395	  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) unit \(\widetilde{(-)}\Gamma\)-iso, equals the algebraic unit of the
1396	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\iota_A\), namely
1397	  \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).

1399	\begin{proof}
1400	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1401	  This is the affine, square-free heart of the computation: there is no pullback square and
1402	  no base-change map yet, only the comparison of two units of two adjunctions related by the
1403	  two tilde dictionaries. The geometric adjunction
1404	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^* \dashv (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\) on
1405	  sheaves of modules transports, under the tilde dictionaries which identify
1406	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*\) of a tilde with extension of scalars
1407	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\) of a tilde
1408	  with restriction of scalars (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}), to the algebraic
1409	  extension/restriction adjunction \((\iota_A^{\,*} \dashv \iota_{A\,*})\) on
1410	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\). The dictionaries being the comparison isomorphisms of these
1411	  adjunctions, naturality identifies the two units; evaluating at \(M\) gives the stated
1412	  value \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1413	\end{proof}

1424	  the canonical tensor inclusions (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}).

1432	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}), transported across the equality
1433	  \(\iota_A \circ \varphi = \iota_{R'} \circ \psi\).

1442	\begin{lemma}[Unit-across-conjugate coherence]
1443	  \label{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
1444	  \lean{CategoryTheory.unit_conjugateEquiv}
1445	  \mathlibok
1446	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1447	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be two
1448	  adjunctions between the same pair of categories, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\), and let
1449	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\) be a natural transformation of the left adjoints. Write
1450	  \(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\) for the conjugation (mate) bijection,
1451	  which carries \(\alpha\) to a natural transformation \(R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\) of the right
1452	  adjoints. Then for every object \(c\),
1453	  \[
1454	    (\eta_1)_c \circ \bigl(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha\bigr)_{L_1 c}
1455	      \;=\; (\eta_2)_c \circ R_2(\alpha_c) ,
1456	  \]
1457	  the transposed form of the conjugation relating the two units across the mate of \(\alpha\).
1458	\end{lemma}

1460	\begin{lemma}[Unit of a composite adjunction]
1461	  \label{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib}
1462	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.comp_unit_app}
1463	  \mathlibok
1464	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1465	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : F \dashv G\) (with \(F : C \to D\)) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : H \dashv I\)
1466	  (with \(H : D \to E\)) be composable adjunctions, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\). Then the
1467	  unit of the composite adjunction
1468	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1 . \mathrm{adj}_2 : (H \circ F) \dashv (G \circ I)\) is, at every object \(X\),
1469	  \[
1470	    \bigl(\mathrm{adj}_1 . \mathrm{adj}_2\bigr)\text{-unit}_X
1471	      \;=\; (\eta_1)_X \circ G\bigl((\eta_2)_{F X}\bigr).
1472	  \]
1473	\end{lemma}

1475	\begin{lemma}[The pullback--pushforward conjugate sends the pullback coherence to the pushforward coherence]
1476	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv}
1478	  \mathlibok
1479	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1480	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) be composable morphisms of schemes, and write
1481	  \((h^{*} \dashv h_{*})\) for the pullback--pushforward adjunction on \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
1482	  attached to a morphism \(h\). Under the conjugation (mate) bijection between the composite
1483	  adjunction \((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*})\) and the adjunction
1484	  \(\bigl((f\circ g)^{*} \dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\), the inverse pullback-composition coherence
1485	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is carried to the pushforward-composition
1486	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{hom}}\):
1487	  \[
1488	    \operatorname{conj}\!\Bigl((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*}),\;
1489	      \bigl((f\circ g)^{*}\dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\Bigr)
1490	      \bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\bigr)
1491	    \;=\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{hom}}.
1492	  \]
1493	\end{lemma}

1495	\begin{lemma}
1496	
1497	\leanok
1498	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1499	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1500	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1501	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1502	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1503	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1504	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1505	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1506	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1507	  composition coherences. Then
1508	  \[
1509	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1510	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1511	    \;=\;
1512	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1513	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1514	  \]
1515	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1516	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1517	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1518	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1519	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1520	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1521	\end{lemma}
1522	\begin{proof}
1523	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1524	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1525	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1526	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1527	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1528	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1529	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1530	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1531	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1532	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1533	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N
1534	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1535	  the stated equation.
1536	\end{proof}

1539	% These three project-bespoke atoms record that, on global sections over Spec R, the two
1540	% pushforward-composition coherences and the pushforward-congruence coherence appearing in
1541	% the inner composite are transparent (their section value is the identity / a presheaf
1542	% transport), hence collapse under the global-sections functor.

1544	\begin{lemma}
1545	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-composition coherence (hom factor)]
1546	  \label{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1547	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1548	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1549	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to \operatorname{Spec} R\) be composable morphisms of
1550	  schemes and \(M\) an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_1}\)-module. Applying the global-sections functor
1551	  \(\Gamma\) (over the base \(R\)) to the component
1552	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\) of the pushforward-composition coherence
1553	  isomorphism yields the identity morphism:
1554	  \[
1555	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\bigr) = \operatorname{id}.
1556	  \]
1557	  The pushforward-composition coherence is transparent at the level of sections.
1558	\end{lemma}
1559	\begin{proof}
1560	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1561	  The component \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\) evaluates to the identity
1562	  on every open, and the global-sections functor sends the identity to the identity.
1563	\end{proof}

1565	\begin{lemma}
1566	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-composition coherence (inv factor)]

1569	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}

1571	  \(\Gamma\)-collapse holds for the inverse component:
1572	  \[
1573	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_M\bigr) = \operatorname{id}.
1574	  \]
1575	\end{lemma}
1576	\begin{proof}
1577	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1578	  The inverse component evaluates to the identity on every open, and the global-sections
1579	  functor preserves the identity.
1580	\end{proof}

1582	\begin{lemma}
1583	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-congruence coherence]

1588	  an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Applying the global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) to the component

1614	  \(g' : X' \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) and \(f' : X' \to \operatorname{Spec} R'\) of the pullback
1615	  square carrying the cone-leg identifications \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\),

1617	  \(w : g' \circ \operatorname{Spec}\varphi = f' \circ \operatorname{Spec}\psi\) as explicit

1629	        lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id, lem:gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom,
1630	        lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value,
1631	        def:base_change_mate_inner_value}

1633	  \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) and \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\):
1634	  the commutativity hypothesis \(w\), the adjunction, and the codomain-read argument
1635	  all transform uniformly under this substitution (step~(i)).

1638	  inverse pushforward-composition factor collapses to the identity

1642	  pushforward-composition hom factor to be handled by step~(iii).

1645	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
1646	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), rewrites the unit factor together with

1905	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), here we read its
1906	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose over \(\operatorname{Spec} R'\), and the result is the
1907	  extension of scalars along \(\psi\) of the Seam~2 value \(\rho\).

1909	  \emph{Counit factorization.} The base-change map
1910	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the adjunction transpose of
1911	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\) with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\). By the
1912	  counit form of the hom-set adjunction (\(\operatorname{homEquiv}\)-counit identity for the
1913	  transpose), it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the counit
1914	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining:
1915	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).
1916	
1917	  \emph{The concrete counit coherence — why it is not a bare computation.} Conjugating
1918	  \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by \(\varepsilon_g\) by the domain identification
1919	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and the codomain
1920	  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read})
1921	  requires the pullback dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\) of
1922	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} — which reads \(g^* = (\operatorname{Spec}\psi)^*\) of a
1923	  tilde as extension of scalars along \(\psi\) — to interact with the geometric counit
1924	  \(\varepsilon_g\). This interaction is \emph{not} a definitional reduction: \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is
1925	  built abstractly as the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints comparison, evaluated from the
1926	  natural-isomorphism of right adjoints \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso})

1933	  \Gamma_{R'})\) is the composite ``extension of scalars then tilde'' adjunction. Because the
1934	  dictionary is opaque in this way, the counit cannot be simplified against it by reflexivity
