1949	  applied to the \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading \(\rho\) of \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\)

950	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
951	
952	\begin{definition}\leanok
953	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
954	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
956	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
957	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
958	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
959	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
960	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
961	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
962	  \begin{itemize}
963	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
964	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
965	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
966	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
967	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
968	    \item a finiteness condition: \(M\) is a finite module over the polynomial ring
969	      \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\).
970	  \end{itemize}
971	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
972	  Hilbert function} is
973	  \[
974	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
975	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
976	  \]
977	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
978	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
979	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
980	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
981	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
982	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
983	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
984	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
985	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
986	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
987	\end{definition}
988	
989	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
990	
991	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
992	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
993	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
994	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
995	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
996	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
997	
998	\begin{definition}[Degree-raising endomorphism]
999	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1000	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1001	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1002	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1003	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1004	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1005	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1006	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1007	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1008	\end{definition}
1009	
1010	\begin{lemma}[Membership form of degree-raising]
1011	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1012	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1013	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1014	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1015	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1016	\end{lemma}
1017	
1018	\begin{proof}
1019	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1020	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1021	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1022	\end{proof}
1023	
1024	\begin{lemma}[Degree-shift commutation]
1025	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1026	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1027	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1028	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1029	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1030	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1031	  \[
1032	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1033	  \]
1034	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1035	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1036	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1037	\end{lemma}
1038	
1039	\begin{proof}
1040	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1041	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1042	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1043	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1044	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1045	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1046	\end{proof}
1047	
1048	\begin{lemma}[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1049	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1050	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1051	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1052	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1053	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1054	\end{lemma}
1055	
1056	\begin{proof}
1057	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1058	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1059	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1060	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1061	\end{proof}
1062	
1063	\begin{lemma}[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1064	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1065	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1066	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1067	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1068	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1069	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1070	\end{lemma}
1071	
1072	\begin{proof}
1073	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1074	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1075	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1076	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1077	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1078	  homogeneous.
1079	\end{proof}
1080	
1081	\begin{lemma}[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1082	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1083	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1084	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1085	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1086	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1087	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1088	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1089	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1090	\end{lemma}
1091	
1092	\begin{proof}
1093	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1094	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1095	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1096	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1097	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1098	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1099	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1100	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1101	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1102	  homogeneous.
1103	\end{proof}
1104	
1105	\begin{lemma}[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1106	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1108	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1109	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1110	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1111	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1112	  this is recorded here.
1113	\end{lemma}
1114	
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1117	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1118	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1119	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1120	\end{proof}
1121	
1122	\begin{lemma}[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1123	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1124	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1125	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1126	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1127	\end{lemma}
1128	
1129	\begin{proof}
1130	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1131	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1132	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1133	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1134	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1135	\end{proof}
1136	
1137	\begin{lemma}[Ambient image identity]
1138	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1140	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1141	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1142	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1143	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1144	  \[
1145	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1146	  \]
1147	\end{lemma}
1148	
1149	\begin{proof}
1150	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1151	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1152	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1153	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1154	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1155	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1156	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1157	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1158	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1159	\end{proof}
1160	
1161	\begin{lemma}[Ambient distributive law]
1162	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1164	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1165	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1166	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1167	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1168	  \[
1169	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1170	  \]
1171	\end{lemma}
1172	
1173	\begin{proof}
1174	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1175	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1176	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1177	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1178	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1179	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1180	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1181	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1182	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1183	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1184	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1185	\end{proof}
1186	
1187	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1188	
1189	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1190	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_ker_coker}
1192	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1193	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1194	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1195	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1196	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1197	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1198	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1199	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1200	  \[
1201	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1202	    \qquad
1203	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1204	  \]
1205	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1206	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1207	  \begin{enumerate}
1208	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1209	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1210	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1211	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1212	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1213	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1214	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1215	  \end{enumerate}
1216	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1217	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1218	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1219	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1220	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1221	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1222	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1223	\end{lemma}
1224	
1225	\begin{proof}
1226	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1227	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1228	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1229	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1230	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1231	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1232	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1233	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1234	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1235	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1236	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1237	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1238	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1239	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1240	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1241	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1242	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1243	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1244	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1245	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1246	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1247	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1248	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1249	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1250	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1251	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1252	
1253	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1254	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1255	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1256	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1257	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1258	
1259	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1260	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1261	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1262	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1263	
1264	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1265	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1266	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1267	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1268	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1269	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1270	\end{proof}
1271	
1272	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1273	
1274	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1275	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1276	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1278	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1279	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1280	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1281	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1282	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1283	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1284	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1285	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1286	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1287	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1288	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1289	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1290	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1292	  \[
1293	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1294	  \]
1295	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1296	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1297	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1298	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1299	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1300	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1301	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1302	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1303	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1304	\end{lemma}
1305	
1306	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1307	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1308	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1309	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1310	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1311	%  following short exact sequence
1312	%  $$
1313	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1314	%  $$
1315	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1316	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1317	\begin{proof}
1318	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1319	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1320	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1321	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1322	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1324	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1325	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1326	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1327	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1328	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1329	
1330	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1331	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1332	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1333	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1334	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1335	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1336	
1337	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1338	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1339	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1340	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1341	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1342	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1343	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1344	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1345	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1346	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1347	
1348	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1349	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1350	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1351	  \[
1352	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1353	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1354	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1355	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1356	  \]
1357	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1358	\end{proof}
1359	
1360	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1361	
1362	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1363	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1365	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1366	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1367	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1368	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1369	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1370	  subquotients
1371	  \[
1372	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1373	  \]
1374	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1375	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1376	  \(r-1\), satisfying the finiteness condition. This is what is needed for the
1377	  inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1378	
1379	  The kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1380	  satisfy the same conditions as the original datum -- a homogeneous pair, pairwise-commuting
1381	  degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, a polynomial-ring module structure, and
1382	  the finiteness condition -- but of length \(r-1\) rather than \(r\).
1383	\end{lemma}
1384	
1385	\begin{proof}
1386	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1387	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1388	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1389	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1390	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1391	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1392	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1393	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1394	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1395	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1396	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1397	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1398	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1399	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\).
1400	
1401	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1402	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1403	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1404	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1405	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1406	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1407	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1408	  polynomial rings
1409	  \[
1410	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1411	  \]
1412	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1413	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1414	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1415	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1416	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1417	
1418	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1419	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1420	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1421	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1422	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1423	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1424	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1425	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients.
1426	\end{proof}
1427	
1428	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1429	
1430	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1431	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1432	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1434	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1435	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1436	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1437	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1438	  \[
1439	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1440	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1441	  \]
1442	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1443	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1444	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1445	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1446	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1447	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1448	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1449	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1450	  \[
1451	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1452	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1453	  \]
1454	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1455	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1456	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1457	\end{lemma}
1458	
1459	\begin{proof}
1460	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1461	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1462	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1463	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1464	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1465	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1466	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1467	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1468	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1469	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1470	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1471	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1472	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1473	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1474	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1475	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1476	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1477	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1478	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1479	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1480	\end{proof}
1481	
1482	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1483	
1484	\begin{lemma}[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1485	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1487	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1488	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1489	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1490	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1491	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1492	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1493	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1494	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1495	  degree-one generators).}
1496	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1497	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1498	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1499	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1500	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1501	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1502	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1503	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1504	  \(M\).
1505	\end{lemma}
1506	
1507	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1508	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1509	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1510	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1511	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1512	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1513	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1514	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1515	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1516	\begin{proof}
1517	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1518	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1519	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1520	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1521	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1522	
1523	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1524	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1525	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1526	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1527	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1528	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1529	
1530	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1531	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1532	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1533	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1534	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1535	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1536	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1537	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1538	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1539	  \[
1540	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1541	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1542	  \]
1543	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
1544	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1545	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1546	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1547	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1548	\end{proof}
1549	
1550	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1551	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1552	
1553	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1554	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1555	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1556	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1557	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1558	
1559	\begin{definition}
1560	
1561	\leanok
1562	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1563	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1565	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1566	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1567	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1568	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1569	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1570	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1571	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1572	  % Polynomials" (read from
1573	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1574	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1575	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1576	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1577	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1578	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1579	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1580	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1581	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1582	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1583	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1584	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1585	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1586	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1587	  \[
1588	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1589	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1590	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1591	  \]
1592	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1593	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1594	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1595	
1596	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1597	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1598	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1599	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1600	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1601	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1602	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1603	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1604	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1605	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1606	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1607	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1608	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1609	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1610	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1611	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1612	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1613	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1614	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1615	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1616	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1617	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1618	
1619	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1620	\end{definition}
1621	
1622	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1623	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
1624	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
1625	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
1626	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
1627	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
1628	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
1629	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
1630	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
1631	  \(U\):
1632	  \[
1633	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1634	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
1635	  \]
1636	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
1637	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
1638	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
1639	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
1640	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
1641	\end{lemma}
1642	
1643	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1644	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1645	  localization]
1646	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1647	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1648	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1649	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1650	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1651	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1652	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1653	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1654	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1655	  \[
1656	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1657	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1658	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1659	  \]
1660	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1661	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1662	
1663	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1664	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1665	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1666	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1667	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1668	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1669	\end{lemma}
1670	
1671	\begin{proof}
1672	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1673	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1674	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1675	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1676	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1677	
1678	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1679	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1680	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1681	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1682	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1683	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1684	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1685	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1686	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1687	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1688	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1689	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1690	\end{proof}

1707	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
1708	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
1710	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1711	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
1712	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
1713	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
1714	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1715	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
1716	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1717	  %  associated to $M$.
1718	  %  [...]
1719	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
1720	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
1721	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
1722	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1723	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1724	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1725	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1726	  \begin{enumerate}
1727	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1728	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1729	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
1730	    \item the section-restriction map
1731	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
1732	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
1733	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
1734	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
1735	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
1736	  \end{enumerate}
1737	\end{lemma}
1738	
1739	\begin{proof}
1740	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1741	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
1742	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
1743	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
1744	
1745	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
1746	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
1747	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
1748	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
1749	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
1750	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
1751	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
1752	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
1753	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
1754	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
1755	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
1756	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
1757	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
1758	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
1759	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
1760	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
1761	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
1762	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
1763	\end{proof}

1812	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
1813	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
1814	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
1815	  \mathlibok
1816	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1817	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
1818	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
1819	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
1820	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
1821	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
1822	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
1823	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
1824	\end{lemma}

2350	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
2351	
2352	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
2353	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
2354	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

2356	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2357	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2358	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2359	  \mathlibok
2360	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2361	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2362	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2363	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2364	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2365	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2366	  \[
2367	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2368	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2369	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2370	  \]
2371	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2372	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2373	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2374	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2375	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2376	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2377	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2378	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2379	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2380	\end{lemma}

2506	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
2507	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
2508	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
2509	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
2510	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
2511	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
2512	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
2513	\begin{proof}
2514	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2515	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
2516	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
2517	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
2518	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
2519	  sequence.
2520	
2521	  \medskip
2522	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
2523	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
2524	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
2525	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
2526	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
2527	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
2528	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
2529	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
2530	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
2531	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
2532	  \[
2533	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
2534	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
2535	  \]
2536	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
2537	
2538	  \medskip
2539	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
2540	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
2541	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
2542	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
2543	  \[
2544	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2545	    \;=\;
2546	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
2547	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
2548	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
2549	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
2550	    \Bigr),
2551	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
2552	  \]
2553	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
2554	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
2555	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2556	  higher cohomology.
2557	
2558	  \medskip
2559	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2560	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2561	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2562	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2563	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2564	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2565	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2566	  \[
2567	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2568	    \qquad
2569	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2570	  \]
2571	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2572	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2573	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2574	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2575	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
2576	  \[
2577	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2578	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2579	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2580	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2581	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2582	  \]
2583	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2584	
2585	  \medskip
2586	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2587	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2588	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2589	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2590	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2591	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2592	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2593	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2594	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2595	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2596	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2597	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2598	  \[
2599	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2600	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2601	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2602	  \]
2603	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2604	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2605	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2606	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2607	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2608	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2609	\end{proof}

893	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the

900	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
901	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
902	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
903	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
904	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
905	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
906	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
907	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
908	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
909	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
910	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
911	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
912	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
913	\end{proof}

931	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:

956	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
957	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
958	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
959	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
960	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
961	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
962	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
963	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
964	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).

967	\begin{proof}
968	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
969	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
970	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
971	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
972	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
973	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
974	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
975	
976	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
977	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
978	
979	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
980	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
981	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
982	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
983	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
984	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
985	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
986	
987	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
988	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
989	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
990	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
991	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
992	  \(A_g\).
993	
994	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
995	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
996	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
997	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
998	
999	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1000	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1001	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1002	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1003	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1004	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1005	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1006	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1007	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1008	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1009	
1010	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1011	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1012	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1013	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1014	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1015	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1016	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1017	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1018	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1019	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1020	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1021	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1022	
1023	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1024	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1025	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1026	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1027	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1028	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1029	
1030	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1031	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1032	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1033	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1034	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1035	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1036	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1037	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1038	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1039	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1040	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1041	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1042	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1043	
1044	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1045	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1046	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1047	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1048	\end{proof}

1050	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1051	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1052	
1053	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1054	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1055	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1056	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1057	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1058	
1059	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1060	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1061	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1062	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1063	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1064	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1065	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1066	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1067	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1068	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1069	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1070	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1071	\end{lemma}
1072	\begin{proof}
1073	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1074	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1075	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1076	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1077	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1078	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1079	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1080	\end{proof}

1082	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1083	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1084	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1085	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1086	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1087	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1088	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1089	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1090	\end{lemma}
1091	\begin{proof}
1092	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1093	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1094	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1095	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1096	\end{proof}

1098	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1099	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1101	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1102	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1103	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1104	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1105	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1106	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1107	\end{lemma}
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1110	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1111	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1112	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1113	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1114	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1115	  these conditions gives the claim.
1116	\end{proof}

1118	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1119	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}

1121	  \mathlibok

1123	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1124	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1125	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1126	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1127	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1128	\end{lemma}

1130	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1131	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}

1133	  \mathlibok

1141	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1142	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1144	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1145	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1146	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1147	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1148	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1149	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1150	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1151	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1152	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1153	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1154	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1155	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1156	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1157	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1158	  % NOTE (iter-016): the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1159	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1160	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1161	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1162	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1163	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1164	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1165	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1166	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1167	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1168	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1169	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1170	\end{lemma}
1171	
1172	\begin{proof}
1173	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1174	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1175	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1176	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1177	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1178	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1179	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1180	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1181	
1182	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1183	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1184	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1185	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1186	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1187	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1188	
1189	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1190	  \[
1191	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1192	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1193	  \]
1194	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1195	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1196	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1197	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1198	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1199	
1200	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1201	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1202	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1203	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1204	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1205	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1206	\end{proof}
